name: RoomAndPreferences
tagline: Persist notes in a Room SQLite table and settings in DataStore so everything survives an app restart.
whatItDoes: You see a single notes screen with a 'Dark theme' switch, a sort-order button ('Sort: Newest' / 'Sort: A-Z'), and a small form with Title and Body fields plus an 'Add note' button. Typing a title/body and tapping Add appends a row to a scrolling list; each row has a done checkbox and a Delete button. Flipping the dark-theme switch instantly re-colors the whole app, and the sort button reorders the list. Crucially, every note you add and every setting you toggle is written to disk, so if you kill the app and relaunch it your notes and your dark-theme choice are still there.
mentalModel: Two complementary persistence tools feed reactive Flows into one ViewModel: Room for structured rows (the notes table) and DataStore for simple key/value settings (dark theme, sort order), and the UI just collects those streams.

screens.name: NotesScreen
screens.role: Stateless screen holding the settings row (dark-theme Switch + sort Button), the add-note form, and the LazyColumn of persisted notes; takes all data and callbacks as parameters so it is previewable.

screens.name: NotesRoute
screens.role: Stateful wrapper that collects the ViewModel's Room and DataStore StateFlows and forwards the data plus action callbacks down into NotesScreen.

screens.name: NoteRow
screens.role: One list row showing a done Checkbox, the title/body text, and a Delete button, delegating all behavior to passed-in callbacks.

concepts.plain: The data class 'Note' is annotated '@Entity(tableName = notes)', which tells Room to generate a real SQLite table named 'notes'. Each property (id, title, body, done) becomes a column and each Note instance becomes a row.
'@PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true) val id: Long = 0' makes id the unique row id; passing 0 on insert tells SQLite to assign the next id automatically.

'NoteDao' is an interface where 'observeNotes(): Flow<List<Note>>' returns a live stream. Room re-runs the SELECT and emits a fresh list every time the notes table changes, so the UI updates with no manual refresh.
insert/update/delete are marked 'suspend' because they touch disk and must run off the main thread; the ViewModel calls them inside 'viewModelScope.launch { }'.

'NoteDatabase.getInstance()' uses '@Volatile' plus double-checked locking around 'Room.databaseBuilder(..., notes.db)' to build exactly one database instance per process, avoiding wasted connections and state corruption.

'SettingsRepository' uses 'preferencesDataStore(name = settings)' to store simple key/value settings. It reads via 'dataStore.data.map { }' (a reactive Flow) and writes via the suspend, transactional 'dataStore.edit { }' block; it is the modern replacement for SharedPreferences.

The ViewModel converts each persistence Flow into a StateFlow with 'stateIn(viewModelScope, SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), initial)', so Compose always has a current value and collection stays tied to the ViewModel lifetime.

'settings.sortOrder.flatMapLatest { order -> ... }' makes the notes Flow re-subscribe to either observeNotes() or observeNotesByTitle() whenever the persisted sort setting changes, so DataStore and Room cooperate reactively.

files.role: The Compose UI: MainActivity drives the theme from the persisted dark-theme flag, plus the stateful NotesRoute, the stateless NotesScreen, NoteRow, and the @Preview functions.
The state holder: turns Room and DataStore Flows into StateFlows and exposes addNote/deleteNote/toggleDone/setDarkTheme/setSortOrder actions, with an Application factory.
The Room @Entity data class describing one row of the 'notes' table (id, title, body, done).
The Room @Dao interface: reactive Flow queries (observeNotes / observeNotesByTitle) plus suspend insert/update/delete.
The @Database holder that lists the Note entity and exposes a thread-safe singleton built with Room.databaseBuilder('notes.db').
The DataStore Preferences layer: SortOrder enum, typed keys, reactive Flow reads, and suspend edit{} writes for dark theme and sort order.
Project overview explaining when to use Room vs DataStore, the KSP wiring, and how to inspect the live database.

highlights.title: The Room @Entity: a Kotlin class that IS a table
highlights.explain: '@Entity(tableName = notes)' tells Room to generate a SQLite table at compile time; each constructor property becomes a column and each Note instance becomes a row. '@PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true)' with 'id: Long = 0' lets SQLite assign the id on insert. The Boolean 'done' is stored as an INTEGER 0/1. This is the structured-data half of the project.

highlights.title: The DAO's reactive Flow query
highlights.explain: 'observeNotes(): Flow<List<Note>>' is the key Room idea: a query returning a Flow is live, so Room re-runs the SELECT and pushes a brand-new list every time the notes table changes. The second query orders by title for the alternate sort. 'insert' is 'suspend' because writing to disk must not block the UI thread, so the ViewModel calls it from a coroutine.

highlights.title: The thread-safe database singleton
highlights.explain: '@Volatile' plus double-checked locking ('INSTANCE ?: synchronized(this) { INSTANCE ?: ... }') guarantees exactly one NoteDatabase is ever built for the 'notes.db' file, even if two threads race at startup. 'Room.databaseBuilder' uses 'applicationContext' to avoid leaking an Activity. Creating multiple instances for the same file is wasteful and can corrupt state, which is why this caching matters.

highlights.title: DataStore: reactive reads + transactional writes
highlights.explain: 'darkTheme: Flow<Boolean>' reads via 'dataStore.data.map { }', emitting a fresh value whenever the stored preference changes, and defaulting to false when the key is absent. 'setDarkTheme' writes inside the suspend, transactional 'dataStore.edit { }' block, which atomically commits to disk. This is the modern replacement for SharedPreferences.edit().putBoolean(...).apply(), the simple-settings half of the project.

highlights.title: flatMapLatest swaps Room queries when the setting changes
highlights.explain: The notes StateFlow observes DataStore's sort-order Flow and uses 'flatMapLatest' to switch to whichever Room query Flow matches (observeNotes for newest-first, observeNotesByTitle for A-Z). Change the persisted setting and a new Room Flow is collected, so the list re-sorts automatically. 'stateIn(viewModelScope, WhileSubscribed(5000), emptyList())' caches the latest value as hot UI state.

highlights.title: Actions: user intents become suspend persistence writes
highlights.explain: Each action launches on 'viewModelScope.launch { }' so the suspend DAO/DataStore write runs off the UI thread. 'addNote' trims and ignores empty titles before calling 'noteDao.insert', 'toggleDone' persists 'note.copy(done = !note.done)' via update, and 'setDarkTheme' calls into the DataStore repository. The UI never writes directly; it only invokes these intents.

highlights.title: Driving the whole theme from the persisted setting
highlights.explain: The Activity obtains the ViewModel via its Application factory, then 'collectAsStateWithLifecycle()' turns the persisted darkTheme StateFlow into Compose state. That value feeds 'RoomAndPreferencesTheme(darkTheme = darkTheme, dynamicColor = false)', so the app re-colors the instant DataStore changes and starts in the saved mode on next launch. NotesRoute then wires the rest of the persistence state and actions into the screen.

newVsPrev: MvvmState taught the MVVM shape: a ViewModel exposing StateFlow UI state that a stateless screen collects, but its state lived only in memory and was lost when the app died. RoomAndPreferences adds real LOCAL PERSISTENCE behind that same MVVM wiring: a Room SQLite database (Note @Entity, NoteDao with reactive Flow queries, NoteDatabase singleton) for structured note rows, and DataStore Preferences (SettingsRepository) for simple settings. It also introduces an AndroidViewModel with an Application factory (needed for a Context), suspend writes launched on viewModelScope, KSP-generated Room code, and flatMapLatest to switch the active query when a persisted setting changes. The takeaway: state now survives an app restart.
tryThis: Add a new column to the Note entity (e.g. 'val pinned: Boolean = false'). Because the schema changed you must bump 'version = 1' to 2 in @Database; for this teaching app the fastest fix is to uninstall the app (or add '.fallbackToDestructiveMigration()' to the databaseBuilder) so Room recreates the table, then rebuild and watch KSP regenerate the code.

Add a third SortOrder value (e.g. OLDEST_FIRST) and a matching @Query 'ORDER BY id ASC' in NoteDao, then extend the when-branches in NotesViewModel.notes and the sort Button label in NotesScreen so the new order persists through DataStore.
Open View > Tool Windows > App Inspection > Database Inspector, select notes.db, and watch rows appear and disappear live as you add, check off, and delete notes.
Toggle the dark-theme switch ON, fully close the app from the recents list, and relaunch it; confirm it opens in dark mode, proving the DataStore value was read back from disk.
Temporarily change 'SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000)' to 'SharingStarted.Eagerly' on one StateFlow and use the debugger to observe how/when the upstream Flow starts collecting.
gotchas: Never build a NotesViewModel inside a @Preview. The previews call the stateless NotesScreen with hand-supplied previewNotes and no-op {} callbacks, because a real ViewModel would try to open the Room database and DataStore, which do not exist in the design pane and would crash the preview.

SortOrder is persisted by its NAME string (order.name) via stringPreferencesKey, not its ordinal. Storing the ordinal would silently break if you ever reorder the enum values; reading back uses runCatching { SortOrder.valueOf(...) } and falls back to NEWEST_FIRST on unknown text.

The Room compiler is added with 'ksp(libs.androidx.room.compiler)', not 'implementation', and the com.google.devtools.ksp plugin must be applied. KSP is what generates NoteDatabase_Impl and NoteDao_Impl at build time; the KSP version is pinned to the Kotlin version, and a mismatch breaks the build.

This sample uses a hand-written ViewModel factory and direct SettingsRepository / NoteDatabase.getInstance() construction rather than a dependency-injection library like Hilt, to keep the persistence concepts front and center.
runTest: From /Users/joepangallo/AndroidStudioProjects/RoomAndPreferences run './gradlew assembleDebug' (this runs KSP and Room codegen) and './gradlew testDebugUnitTest' for the example unit test. Install with './gradlew installDebug' or Run in Android Studio. To verify persistence: add a few notes, check some off, flip the dark-theme switch ON, then fully close and reopen the app and confirm the notes and the dark mode are still there. For a live view, open View > Tool Windows > App Inspection > Database Inspector, select notes.db, and watch rows change as you add and delete notes.
